hero.bio: I use data and machine learning to understand how people learn, and build tools that actually help them do it better.
hero.currently: OAK Lab Research Assistant; DFA Team Lead

professional: I'm a junior at Carnegie Mellon studying Statistics and Machine Learning. Most of my work sits at the intersection of data and human behavior. I'm interested in what the numbers reveal about how people think, learn, and make decisions, and what you can actually do with that.

Right now I'm a research assistant at the OAK Lab building ML pipelines to analyze how learners discover rules. I'm looking for opportunities in data science, ML, or AI broadly. EdTech is where I've spent most of my time, but I'm genuinely interested in any domain where data can do something useful.
about.beyond: Outside of work, I enjoy singing and traveling.

items.sub: pandas, NumPy, scikit-learn, PyTorch, TensorFlow, OpenCV, MediaPipe

projects.title: Learner Engagement Detection System
projects.desc: Real-time facial Action Unit detection from 478 MediaPipe landmarks to classify learner engagement and trigger adaptive feedback.

projects.title: Bay Area Bike Share Demand Prediction
projects.desc: Trained a machine learning model on 360k+ trip records to predict hourly bike demand across 40+ stations, hitting 70.21% accuracy.

projects.title: K-12 Career Education Platform: UX Research
projects.desc: Mixed-methods research with school counselors and district decision-makers to surface why career ed software doesn't get adopted.

projects.desc: Designed and built this site from scratch using plain HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. Hosted on Firebase with a custom domain.

research.title: Inside blocked practice: Verbal evidence for the attentional mechanisms driving category learning
research.authors: Julia J. Conti, Abigail Lo, Ken R. Koedinger, Paulo F. Carvalho
research.desc: Blocked presentation of information is thought to provide benefits over interleaving by directing attention to commonalities. However, most current evidence is indirect, in the form of posttest performance and eyetracking. In this study, participants learned to classify bugs organized around a relational rule in a 2x2 design manipulating blocked vs. interleaved presentation and example presence vs. absence. Throughout the task, we ask participants to make predictions about the organizing rule. Blocked presentation promoted better post-test performance and more relational feature verbalizations than interleaved; removing examples amplified feature use in both conditions. Moreover, greater increases in relational verbalizations during training positively predicted posttest performance, particularly for blocked learners, suggesting presentation format shapes reasoning trajectories.
